question:
Let $c>0$ be a constant. Give a complete description, with proof, of
the set of all continuous functions $f: R \to R$ such that $f(x) =
f(x^2+c)$ for all $x \in R$. Note that $R$ denotes the set of real numbers.
solution:
We first consider the case $c \leq 1/4$; we shall show in this case
$f$ must be constant. The relation
\[
f(x) = f(x^2 + c) = f((-x)^2 + c) = f(-x)
\]
proves that $f$ is an even function. Let $r_1 \leq r_2$ be the roots of
$x^2 + c - x$, both of which are real. If $x > r_{2}$, define $x_{0} =
x$ and $x_{n+1} = \sqrt{x_{n} - c}$ for each positive integer $x$. By
induction on $n$, $r_{2} < x_{n+1} < x_{n}$ for all $n$, so the
sequence $\{x_{n}\}$ tends to a limit $L$ which is a root of $x^{2} +
c = x$ not less than $r_{2}$. Of course this means $L = r_{2}$.
Since $f(x) = f(x_{n})$ for all $n$ and $x_{n} \to r_{2}$, we
conclude $f(x) = f(r_{2})$, so $f$ is constant on $x \geq r_{2}$.

If $r_{1} < x < r_{2}$ and $x_{n}$ is defined as before, then by
induction, $x_{n} < x_{n+1} < r_{2}$. Note that the
sequence can be defined because $r_{1} > c$; the latter follows by
noting that the polynomial $x^{2} - x + c$ is positive at $x = c$ and
has its minimum at $1/2 > c$, so both roots are greater than $c$. In
any case, we deduce that $f(x)$ is also constant on $r_{1} \leq x \leq
r_{2}$.

Finally, suppose $x < r_{1}$. Now define $x_{0} = x, x_{n+1} =
x_{n}^{2} + c$. Given that $x_{n} < r_{1}$, we have $x_{n+1} >
x_{n}$. Thus if we had $x_{n} < r_{1}$ for all $n$, by the same argument as
in the first case we deduce $x_{n} \to r_{1}$ and so $f(x) =
f(r_{1})$. Actually, this doesn't happen; eventually we have $x_{n} >
r_{1}$, in which case $f(x) = f(x_{n}) = f(r_{1})$ by what we have
already shown. We conclude that $f$ is a constant function. (Thanks
to Marshall Buck for catching an inaccuracy in a previous version of
this solution.)

Now suppose $c > 1/4$. Then the sequence $x_n$ defined by $x_0 = 0$
and $x_{n+1} = x_n^2 + c$ is strictly increasing and has no limit
point. Thus if we define $f$ on $[x_0, x_1]$ as any continuous
function with equal values on the endpoints, and extend the definition
from $[x_n, x_{n+1}]$ to $[x_{n+1}, x_{n+2}]$ by the relation $f(x) =
f(x^2 + c)$, and extend the definition further to $x < 0$ by the
relation $f(x) = f(-x)$, the resulting function has the desired
property. Moreover, any function with that property clearly has this form.

descriptive_long.question:
Let $posconstant>0$ be a constant. Give a complete description, with proof, of
the set of all continuous functions $functionf: realset \to realset$ such that $functionf(variablex) =
functionf(variablex^2+posconstant)$ for all $variablex \in realset$. Note that $realset$ denotes the set of real numbers.
descriptive_long.solution:
We first consider the case $posconstant \leq 1/4$; we shall show in this case
$functionf$ must be constant. The relation
\[
functionf(variablex) = functionf(variablex^2 + posconstant) = functionf((-variablex)^2 + posconstant) = functionf(-variablex)
\]
proves that $functionf$ is an even function. Let $rootone \leq roottwo$ be the roots of
$variablex^2 + posconstant - variablex$, both of which are real. If $variablex > roottwo$, define $seedpoint =
variablex$ and $nextseqelem = \sqrt{sequenceelem - posconstant}$ for each positive integer $indexvar$. By
induction on $indexvar$, $roottwo < nextseqelem < sequenceelem$ for all $indexvar$, so the
sequence $\{sequenceelem\}$ tends to a limit $limitvalu$ which is a root of $variablex^{2} +
posconstant = variablex$ not less than $roottwo$. Of course this means $limitvalu = roottwo$.
Since $functionf(variablex) = functionf(sequenceelem)$ for all $indexvar$ and $sequenceelem \to roottwo$, we
conclude $functionf(variablex) = functionf(roottwo)$, so $functionf$ is constant on $variablex \geq roottwo$.

If $rootone < variablex < roottwo$ and $sequenceelem$ is defined as before, then by
induction, $sequenceelem < nextseqelem < roottwo$. Note that the
sequence can be defined because $rootone > posconstant$; the latter follows by
noting that the polynomial $variablex^{2} - variablex + posconstant$ is positive at $variablex = posconstant$ and
has its minimum at $1/2 > posconstant$, so both roots are greater than $posconstant$. In
any case, we deduce that $functionf(variablex)$ is also constant on $rootone \leq variablex \leq
roottwo$.

Finally, suppose $variablex < rootone$. Now define $seedpoint = variablex, nextseqelem =
sequenceelem^{2} + posconstant$. Given that $sequenceelem < rootone$, we have $nextseqelem >
sequenceelem$. Thus if we had $sequenceelem < rootone$ for all $indexvar$, by the same argument as
in the first case we deduce $sequenceelem \to rootone$ and so $functionf(variablex) =
functionf(rootone)$. Actually, this doesnt happen; eventually we have $sequenceelem >
rootone$, in which case $functionf(variablex) = functionf(sequenceelem) = functionf(rootone)$ by what we have
already shown. We conclude that $functionf$ is a constant function. (Thanks
to Marshall Buck for catching an inaccuracy in a previous version of
this solution.)

Now suppose $posconstant > 1/4$. Then the sequence $sequenceelem$ defined by $seedpoint = 0$
and $nextseqelem = sequenceelem^{2} + posconstant$ is strictly increasing and has no limit
point. Thus if we define $functionf$ on $[seedpoint, nextseqelem]$ as any continuous
function with equal values on the endpoints, and extend the definition
from $[sequenceelem, nextseqelem]$ to $[nextseqelem, x_{n+2}]$ by the relation $functionf(variablex) = functionf(variablex^2 + posconstant)$, and extend the definition further to $variablex < 0$ by the
relation $functionf(variablex) = functionf(-variablex)$, the resulting function has the desired
property. Moreover, any function with that property clearly has this form.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $tumbledew>0$ be a constant. Give a complete description, with proof, of
the set of all continuous functions $wanderer: rainfield \to rainfield$ such that $wanderer(marblestep) =
wanderer(marblestep^2+tumbledew)$ for all $marblestep \in rainfield$. Note that $rainfield$ denotes the set of real numbers.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
We first consider the case $tumbledew \leq 1/4$; we shall show in this case
$wanderer$ must be constant. The relation
\[
wanderer(marblestep) = wanderer(marblestep^2 + tumbledew) = wanderer((-marblestep)^2 + tumbledew) = wanderer(-marblestep)
\]
proves that $wanderer$ is an even function. Let $thistleday \leq ivorymist$ be the roots of
$marblestep^2 + tumbledew - marblestep$, both of which are real. If $marblestep > ivorymist$, define $rosestone =
marblestep$ and $harborbay = \sqrt{glenbrook - tumbledew}$ for each positive integer $lilypatch$. By
induction on $lilypatch$, $ivorymist < harborbay < glenbrook$ for all $lilypatch$, so the
sequence $\{glenbrook\}$ tends to a limit $mistletoe$ which is a root of $marblestep^{2} +
tumbledew = marblestep$ not less than $ivorymist$. Of course this means $mistletoe = ivorymist$.
Since $wanderer(marblestep) = wanderer(glenbrook)$ for all $lilypatch$ and $glenbrook \to ivorymist$, we
conclude $wanderer(marblestep) = wanderer(ivorymist)$, so $wanderer$ is constant on $marblestep \geq ivorymist$.

If $thistleday < marblestep < ivorymist$ and $glenbrook$ is defined as before, then by
induction, $glenbrook < harborbay < ivorymist$. Note that the
sequence can be defined because $thistleday > tumbledew$; the latter follows by
noting that the polynomial $marblestep^{2} - marblestep + tumbledew$ is positive at $marblestep = tumbledew$ and
has its minimum at $1/2 > tumbledew$, so both roots are greater than $tumbledew$. In
any case, we deduce that $wanderer(marblestep)$ is also constant on $thistleday \leq marblestep \leq
ivorymist$.

Finally, suppose $marblestep < thistleday$. Now define $rosestone = marblestep, harborbay =
glenbrook^{2} + tumbledew$. Given that $glenbrook < thistleday$, we have $harborbay >
glenbrook$. Thus if we had $glenbrook < thistleday$ for all $lilypatch$, by the same argument as
in the first case we deduce $glenbrook \to thistleday$ and so $wanderer(marblestep) =
wanderer(thistleday)$. Actually, this doesn't happen; eventually we have $glenbrook >
thistleday$, in which case $wanderer(marblestep) = wanderer(glenbrook) = wanderer(thistleday)$ by what we have
already shown. We conclude that $wanderer$ is a constant function. (Thanks
to Marshall Buck for catching an inaccuracy in a previous version of
this solution.)

Now suppose $tumbledew > 1/4$. Then the sequence $glenbrook$ defined by $rosestone = 0$
and $harborbay = glenbrook^2 + tumbledew$ is strictly increasing and has no limit
point. Thus if we define $wanderer$ on $[rosestone, glenbrook]$ as any continuous
function with equal values on the endpoints, and extend the definition
from $[glenbrook, harborbay]$ to $[harborbay, x_{n+2}]$ by the relation $wanderer(marblestep) =
wanderer(marblestep^2 + tumbledew)$, and extend the definition further to $marblestep < 0$ by the
relation $wanderer(marblestep) = wanderer(-marblestep)$, the resulting function has the desired
property. Moreover, any function with that property clearly has this form.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $\variableparam>0$ be a constant. Give a complete description, with proof, of
the set of all continuous functions $\discontinuousmap: \imaginaryset \to \imaginaryset$ such that $\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue) =
\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue^2+\variableparam)$ for all $\constantvalue \in \imaginaryset$. Note that $\imaginaryset$ denotes the set of real numbers.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
We first consider the case $\variableparam \leq 1/4$; we shall show in this case
$\discontinuousmap$ must be constant. The relation
\[
\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue) = \discontinuousmap(\constantvalue^2 + \variableparam) = \discontinuousmap((-\constantvalue)^2 + \variableparam) = \discontinuousmap(-\constantvalue)
\]
proves that $\discontinuousmap$ is an even function. Let $\peakfirst \leq \peaksecond$ be the roots of
$\constantvalue^2 + \variableparam - \constantvalue$, both of which are real. If $\constantvalue > \peaksecond$, define $\terminalvalue =
\constantvalue$ and $\previousterm = \sqrt{\finalterm - \variableparam}$ for each positive integer $\constantvalue$. By
induction on $\nonindex$, $\peaksecond < \previousterm < \finalterm$ for all $\nonindex$, so the
sequence $\{\finalterm\}$ tends to a limit $\divergentval$ which is a root of $\constantvalue^{2} +
\variableparam = \constantvalue$ not less than $\peaksecond$. Of course this means $\divergentval = \peaksecond$.
Since $\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue) = \discontinuousmap(\finalterm)$ for all $\nonindex$ and $\finalterm \to \peaksecond$, we
conclude $\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue) = \discontinuousmap(\peaksecond)$, so $\discontinuousmap$ is constant on $\constantvalue \geq \peaksecond$.

If $\peakfirst < \constantvalue < \peaksecond$ and $\finalterm$ is defined as before, then by
induction, $\finalterm < \previousterm < \peaksecond$. Note that the
sequence can be defined because $\peakfirst > \variableparam$; the latter follows by
noting that the polynomial $\constantvalue^{2} - \constantvalue + \variableparam$ is positive at $\constantvalue = \variableparam$ and
has its minimum at $1/2 > \variableparam$, so both roots are greater than $\variableparam$. In
any case, we deduce that $\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue)$ is also constant on $\peakfirst \leq \constantvalue \leq
\peaksecond$.

Finally, suppose $\constantvalue < \peakfirst$. Now define $\terminalvalue = \constantvalue, \previousterm =
\finalterm^{2} + \variableparam$. Given that $\finalterm < \peakfirst$, we have $\previousterm >
\finalterm$. Thus if we had $\finalterm < \peakfirst$ for all $\nonindex$, by the same argument as
in the first case we deduce $\finalterm \to \peakfirst$ and so $\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue) =
\discontinuousmap(\peakfirst)$. Actually, this doesn't happen; eventually we have $\finalterm >
\peakfirst$, in which case $\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue) = \discontinuousmap(\finalterm) = \discontinuousmap(\peakfirst)$ by what we have
already shown. We conclude that $\discontinuousmap$ is a constant function. (Thanks
to Marshall Buck for catching an inaccuracy in a previous version of
this solution.)

Now suppose $\variableparam > 1/4$. Then the sequence $\finalterm$ defined by $\terminalvalue = 0$
and $\previousterm = \finalterm^2 + \variableparam$ is strictly increasing and has no limit
point. Thus if we define $\discontinuousmap$ on $[\terminalvalue, \previousterm]$ as any continuous
function with equal values on the endpoints, and extend the definition
from $[\finalterm, \previousterm]$ to $[\previousterm, \previousterm]$ by the relation $\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue) =
\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue^2 + \variableparam)$, and extend the definition further to $\constantvalue < 0$ by the
relation $\discontinuousmap(\constantvalue) = \discontinuousmap(-\constantvalue)$, the resulting function has the desired
property. Moreover, any function with that property clearly has this form.

garbled_string.question:
Let $asdlkqwe>0$ be a constant. Give a complete description, with proof, of
the set of all continuous functions $qzxwvtnp: kjtrplmn \to kjtrplmn$ such that $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) =
qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla^2+asdlkqwe)$ for all $hjgrksla \in kjtrplmn$. Note that $kjtrplmn$ denotes the set of real numbers.
garbled_string.solution:
We first consider the case $asdlkqwe \leq 1/4$; we shall show in this case
$qzxwvtnp$ must be constant. The relation
\[
qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) = qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla^{2} + asdlkqwe) = qzxwvtnp((-hjgrksla)^{2} + asdlkqwe) = qzxwvtnp(-hjgrksla)
\]
proves that $qzxwvtnp$ is an even function. Let $plksomnv \leq vczyopql$ be the roots of
$hjgrksla^{2} + asdlkqwe - hjgrksla$, both of which are real. If $hjgrksla > vczyopql$, define $mndkzqpx =
hjgrksla$ and $wqpsldkj = \sqrt{rgfltvza - asdlkqwe}$ for each positive integer $hjgrksla$. By
induction on $vbqrplwt$, $vczyopql < wqpsldkj < rgfltvza$ for all $vbqrplwt$, so the
sequence $\{rgfltvza\}$ tends to a limit $cznvmbqt$ which is a root of $hjgrksla^{2} +
asdlkqwe = hjgrksla$ not less than $vczyopql$. Of course this means $cznvmbqt = vczyopql$.
Since $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) = qzxwvtnp(rgfltvza)$ for all $vbqrplwt$ and $rgfltvza \to vczyopql$, we
conclude $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) = qzxwvtnp(vczyopql)$, so $qzxwvtnp$ is constant on $hjgrksla \geq vczyopql$.

If $plksomnv < hjgrksla < vczyopql$ and $rgfltvza$ is defined as before, then by
induction, $rgfltvza < wqpsldkj < vczyopql$. Note that the
sequence can be defined because $plksomnv > asdlkqwe$; the latter follows by
noting that the polynomial $hjgrksla^{2} - hjgrksla + asdlkqwe$ is positive at $hjgrksla = asdlkqwe$ and
has its minimum at $1/2 > asdlkqwe$, so both roots are greater than $asdlkqwe$. In
any case, we deduce that $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla)$ is also constant on $plksomnv \leq hjgrksla \leq
vczyopql$.

Finally, suppose $hjgrksla < plksomnv$. Now define $mndkzqpx = hjgrksla, wqpsldkj =
rgfltvza^{2} + asdlkqwe$. Given that $rgfltvza < plksomnv$, we have $wqpsldkj > rgfltvza$.
Thus if we had $rgfltvza < plksomnv$ for all $vbqrplwt$, by the same argument as
in the first case we deduce $rgfltvza \to plksomnv$ and so $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) =
qzxwvtnp(plksomnv)$. Actually, this doesn't happen; eventually we have $rgfltvza >
plksomnv$, in which case $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) = qzxwvtnp(rgfltvza) = qzxwvtnp(plksomnv)$ by what we have
already shown. We conclude that $qzxwvtnp$ is a constant function. (Thanks
to Marshall Buck for catching an inaccuracy in a previous version of
this solution.)

Now suppose $asdlkqwe > 1/4$. Then the sequence $rgfltvza$ defined by $mndkzqpx = 0$
and $wqpsldkj = rgfltvza^{2} + asdlkqwe$ is strictly increasing and has no limit
point. Thus if we define $qzxwvtnp$ on $[mndkzqpx, x_1]$ as any continuous
function with equal values on the endpoints, and extend the definition
from $[rgfltvza, wqpsldkj]$ to $[wqpsldkj, x_{n+2}]$ by the relation $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) =
qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla^{2} + asdlkqwe)$, and extend the definition further to $hjgrksla < 0$ by the
relation $qzxwvtnp(hjgrksla) = qzxwvtnp(-hjgrksla)$, the resulting function has the desired
property. Moreover, any function with that property clearly has this form.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $k>0$ be fixed.  Describe, with proof, all continuous functions $f:
\mathbb R\to\mathbb R$ that satisfy
\[
          f(x)=f\bigl(x^{2}+2k\bigr)\qquad\text{for every }x\in\mathbb R.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
We want all continuous f:\mathbb{R}\to \mathbb{R} satisfying
    f(x)=f(x^2+2k)
for fixed k>0.

Define the map T(x)=x^2+2k.  Then f(x)=f(T(x)), and since T(-x)=T(x) it follows immediately that f is even.  Hence it suffices to describe f on [0,\infty ).  The key distinction is whether the equation x=T(x) has real roots.

1.  The fixed-point equation x=x^2+2k \Leftrightarrow  x^2-x+2k=0 has discriminant \Delta =1-8k.
   - If k\leq 1/8 then \Delta \geq 0 and there are two real roots
       r_1=(1-\sqrt{1-8k})/2  \leq   r_2=(1+\sqrt{1-8k})/2.
   - If k>1/8 then \Delta <0 and there are no real solutions.

Case I: k\leq 1/8.  We show f is constant.
   (a)  For any x_0>r_2 define the backward orbit
         x_{n+1}=\sqrt{x_n-2k}.
       One checks x_n>r_2 for all n and x_n\searrow r_2.  Since f(x_n)=f(T(x_{n+1}))=f(x_{n+1}), continuity gives f(x_0)=lim f(x_n)=f(r_2).  Hence f is constant on [r_2,\infty ).
   (b)  For x\in (r_1,r_2) the forward iterates x\mapsto T(x) strictly decrease to r_1, and the same argument shows f(x)=f(r_1).  Continuity at r_2 forces f(r_1)=f(r_2).
   (c)  For x<r_1 one checks T(x)>x and either eventually T^n(x)\geq r_1 or else T^n(x)\nearrow r_1, so f(x)=f(r_1).  Evenness then covers x<0.  Thus f is constant on \mathbb{R}.

Conclusion of Case I: if 0<k\leq 1/8, the only continuous solutions are constant functions.

Case II: k>1/8.  Then x=T(x) has no real solutions and the forward orbit of every x\geq 0 tends to +\infty .  Set x_0=2k, x_{n+1}=x_n^2+2k, so (x_n) is strictly increasing and unbounded.  Write
      I_n=[x_n,x_{n+1}],   n=0,1,2,\ldots 
These intervals cover [2k,\infty ).  Now:
   (A)  Arbitrarily choose a continuous g on I_0=[2k,4k^2+2k] with g(x_0)=g(x_1).
   (B)  For each n\geq 1 and x\in I_n, define f(x)=g(T^{-n}(x)), where T^{-n}(x) is the unique backward-iterate obtained by applying the branch x\mapsto \sqrt{x-2k} exactly n times so as to land in I_0.  This makes f continuous on I_n and satisfies f(T(x))=f(x).
   (C)  If 0\leq x<2k then T(x)=x^2+2k\in I_0, so set f(x)=f(T(x))=g(T(x)), which is continuous up to x=2k.
   (D)  Finally extend to x<0 by f(x)=f(-x), preserving evenness and continuity.

One checks directly that this f is continuous on \mathbb{R}, is even, and obeys f(x)=f(T(x)) for all x.  Conversely, any continuous solution must be constant along each backward orbit under T, so its restriction to I_0 is an arbitrary continuous g with matching endpoint values.  Thus for k>1/8 exactly the above infinite-dimensional family arises.

FINAL ANSWER:
* If 0<k\leq 1/8, the only continuous solutions of f(x)=f(x^2+2k) are the constant functions.
* If k>1/8, pick any continuous g on [2k,4k^2+2k] satisfying g(2k)=g(4k^2+2k), and extend to x\geq 0 by the rule f(x)=g(T^{-n}(x)) whenever x lies in [x_n,x_{n+1}], then set f(x)=f(-x) for x<0.  These and only these are the continuous solutions.

core_steps: Evenness: from f(x)=f(x^2+c)=f((-x)^2+c) we get f(x)=f(-x).
Quadratic split: study roots of x^2+c=x; real roots exist iff c≤1/4, giving two numbers r1≤r2.

Case c≤1/4: build monotone sequences by the maps x↦√(x−c) (for x>r2) and x↦x^2+c (for x<r1); each sequence converges to a root, so continuity forces f to be constant on successive regions and hence on all ℝ.

Case c>1/4: iterates of T(x)=x^2+c from a fixed positive seed give a strictly increasing, unbounded sequence; the intervals [x_n,x_{n+1}] partition the non-negative reals, so prescribing f continuously on the first interval (with equal endpoint values) and extending by the functional equation plus evenness yields every solution.

slot1.description: Choice of the initial seed used to generate the increasing sequence in the c>1/4 case; any non-negative value works.

slot2.description: Interval on which the arbitrary continuous data are first assigned before being extended by the functional equation.
